title: Rocky road slice
subtitle: Chocolate, marshmallow, and biscuit
excerpt: A traybake of dark chocolate, butter, golden syrup, broken biscuits, miniature marshmallows, and raisins. No oven required. Set in the fridge and sliced into rectangles.
sourceNotes: Rocky road is an American confection of the 1920s, originally an ice cream flavour. The British traybake version (fridge-set rather than baked) entered domestic cookery in the mid-twentieth century. This version follows the standard British domestic formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze wrapped individual pieces. Thaw in the fridge for 2 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in the fridge for up to a week. The chocolate sets harder on day two.

glossaryTerms.definition: A method of melting or cooking over indirect heat: a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, without the bowl touching the water. Used for chocolate, custard, and delicate sauces that would scorch over direct heat.

content.text: Rocky road needs no oven and almost no skill, but the ratio of chocolate to filler still matters. Too much biscuit and the slice crumbles and tastes floury; too little and it is pure confectionery. This recipe keeps the biscuit and marshmallow at 60% of the total weight, leaving the chocolate as the structure and flavour.
Line a 20×30 cm tin with baking paper.
Melt the {{dark-chocolate}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, and {{golden-syrup}} in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water using the
method. Stir until smooth, then remove from the heat and stir in the {{fine-sea-salt}}. Allow to cool for 5 minutes.
Fold the broken {{digestive-biscuits}}, {{mini-marshmallows}}, and {{raisins}} into the chocolate mixture until everything is evenly coated. Tip into the tin and press into an even layer.
Refrigerate for at least 2 hours until completely set. Lift out by the paper, place on a board, and cut into 16 pieces. Store in the fridge.
